highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.215 Released
highlights.summary: Claude Code v2.1.215 changes how code review and verification work: the `/verify` and `/code-review` skills no longer run automatically. You now need to invoke them explicitly by typing `/verify` or `/code-review` when you want that analysis, giving you more control over when these checks happen.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.214 Released – Important Security & Permission Fixes
highlights.summary: This release patches two significant security bugs: a permission bypass for Windows PowerShell 5.1 users, and a glob rule bug where `Edit(src/**)` was incorrectly auto-approving writes to nested directories anywhere in the file tree instead of only under your current working directory. If you use allow-rules for file permissions or work on Windows, updating to this version is strongly recommended.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.212 Released – /fork Now Creates Background Sessions
highlights.summary: The `/fork` command has been redesigned: it now copies your current conversation into a new independent background session visible in `claude agents`, so you can branch off work without interrupting your main flow. The old in-session subagent behavior has been moved to the new `/subtask` command, so update your muscle memory if you relied on `/fork` for quick inline tasks.

highlights.title: Claude Code Uses Bun Written in Rust Now
highlights.summary: Simon Willison covers the notable architectural shift in Claude Code, which now runs on Bun (a fast JavaScript runtime) with Rust under the hood. This change has performance implications for how Claude Code starts up and executes tools, and is worth understanding if you're curious about the internals or debugging performance issues.

highlights.title: Setting Up Your Spare Mac for Claude Code to Control – Step-by-Step Guide
highlights.summary: This practical guide walks you through turning a spare Mac into a machine that Claude Code can fully control remotely, enabling powerful automation scenarios. It's a step-by-step walkthrough ideal for developers who want to experiment with Claude Code's computer-use capabilities without risking their primary development machine.

highlights.title: Claude Code: Anatomy of a Misfeature
highlights.summary: This opinion piece critically examines a specific Claude Code behavior the author considers poorly designed, offering a thoughtful analysis of where AI coding assistant UX can go wrong. It's a useful read for developers who want to think critically about how they integrate Claude Code into their workflow and avoid common pitfalls.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: A concise YouTube tutorial designed to get you productive with Claude Code quickly, covering the core features and workflows in just half an hour. This is a great starting point if you prefer video learning and want a focused overview without committing to a multi-hour course.

highlights.title: Claude Code – Full Tutorial for Beginners
highlights.summary: A comprehensive beginner-focused video tutorial that walks through Claude Code from the ground up, making it accessible for developers who are just getting started with AI-assisted coding. Watching this alongside the official docs is a solid way to build foundational habits with Claude Code in VS Code.

tips_of_the_day: Now that `/verify` and `/code-review` are manual-only in v2.1.215, make it a habit to run `/code-review` before committing changes — treating it like a lightweight PR review step can catch issues early without adding noise to your normal coding flow.

If you use file permission allow-rules like `Edit(src/**)`, double-check your glob patterns after updating to v2.1.214. Test that rules only match the directories you intend by running a quick write outside the expected path and confirming Claude Code prompts for approval instead of auto-approving.

Use the new `/fork` command to safely explore risky refactors: fork your current session into a background agent, let it attempt the changes, then review the result in `claude agents` before merging anything back — your main session stays clean and uninterrupted the whole time.
